coredata.dc:title: Valorization of olive pruning lignocellulosic residues into carbon dots: A sustainable approach for discrimination of Iron species

coredata.dc:description: Herein, carbon dots (CDs) obtained from biochar of olive tree pruning residues (BC-OTPR) were synthesized at varying pyrolysis temperatures (500, 600, and 700 °C). The spectroscopic features of the CDs were further explored in the context of metal ion detection, specifically focusing on the discrimination of iron species. Significant fluorescence quenching upon interaction with iron (III), compared to iron (II) and other metal ions, was observed, indicating their potential as sensitive probes. In the presence of hydrogen peroxide and via the Fenton reaction, iron (II) was quantitatively oxidized into iron (III) – the determinable form, allowing for iron speciation. Synthesized CDs were analyzed using UV–Vis spectroscopy. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images revealed the uniform spherical morphology. Using the FT-IR analysis, transformation of hemicellulose and lignin present in the BC-OTPR into CDs with unique functionalities was corroborated. The CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, showcasing their potential for spectroscopic applications. Regression modeling was employed to investigate variables influencing iron detection. Notably, the CDs displayed linear behavior spanning over a range of 1.186 μM – 7.984 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.5 μM of iron (III). Validation of precision and accuracy confirmed CDs' efficacy for analyzing food supplements and water samples.

As per the shown inset CDs-BC-OTPR500 possesses the highest fluorescence intensity compared to the 600 and 700 – counterparts.

Additionally, EDX analysis results are included for CDs-BC-OTPR500 (l), CDs-BC-OTPR600 (m), and CDs-BC-OTPR700 (n).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, carbon dots (CDs) obtained from biochar of olive tree pruning residues (BC-OTPR) were synthesized at varying pyrolysis temperatures (500, 600, and 700 °C). The spectroscopic features of the CDs were further explored in the context of metal ion detection, specifically focusing on the discrimination of iron species. Significant fluorescence quenching upon interaction with iron (III), compared to iron (II) and other metal ions, was observed, indicating their potential as sensitive probes. In the presence of hydrogen peroxide and via the Fenton reaction, iron (II) was quantitatively oxidized into iron (III) – the determinable form, allowing for iron speciation. Synthesized CDs were analyzed using UV–Vis spectroscopy. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images revealed the uniform spherical morphology. Using the FT-IR analysis, transformation of hemicellulose and lignin present in the BC-OTPR into CDs with unique functionalities was corroborated. The CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, showcasing their potential for spectroscopic applications. Regression modeling was employed to investigate variables influencing iron detection. Notably, the CDs displayed linear behavior spanning over a range of 1.186 μM – 7.984 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.5 μM of iron (III). Validation of precision and accuracy confirmed CDs' efficacy for analyzing food supplements and water samples.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Food supplements and water sample analysis

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron, the fourth most prevalent metal on Earth, is a trace element that holds significant importance in various metabolic processes [1,2]. Industrialization, metal corrosion, and the release of industrial effluents into water bodies contribute to the elevated iron levels. High iron concentrations exceeding the maximum permissible threshold of 0.3 mg/L in drinking water can trigger critical health issues, including neuroinflammation, cardiac dysfunction, and hemosiderosis [3–6]. On the other hand, iron deficiency is a common nutritional ailment, with anemia affecting more than two billion people worldwide, particularly in underdeveloped regions, impacting both health and productivity [7].

Transition metals, in general, can exist in different formats (e.g., different oxidation states, organic/inorganic molecular structures, etc.) depending on the surrounding environmental conditions. By and large, the toxicity and functionability of the transition metal are species-dependent [8]. Iron, in its two redox states, iron (II) and iron (III), is integral to various biological and pathological processes and serves as an indicator of cellular redox status [9]. In vivo, iron has the ability to redox cycle and hence alternate between the two oxidation states. In a parallel pathway, iron (II) could catalyze the Fenton reaction with the generation of toxic oxidative radicals that negatively affect the cell membrane and the DNA [10,11]. In the same itinerary, management of iron deficiency anemia is commonly approached using oral iron, with iron (II) formulations being more preferred compared to iron (III), thanks to their better bioavailability and adequate tolerability. The preference for iron (II) over iron (III) formulations remains controversial [12–14]. Yet, and akin to the situation in vivo, oxidation of iron (II) to iron (III) during the manufacturing and storage is likely to happen, triggering the generation of detrimental oxidative species [15].

These variations in impact and toxicity between iron (II) and (III) states imply the need for meticulous regulation of the existing iron species [16]. Literature survey shows the application of several instrumental techniques for the analysis and speciation of iron (II) and iron (III). On top of these techniques, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) coupled to other techniques such as ion chromatography (IC-ICP-OES) [17], and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC-ICP-OES) have been widely used [18,19]. Other techniques such as capillary electrophoresis [20,21], colorimetric sensors [22,23], sequential injection analysis and chemiluminescence [24], and cathodic stripping voltammetry [25] have also been reported. Most of these techniques, however, require extensive pretreatment procedures, and a sort of high-tech instruments that in turn require skilled personnel and an expensive setup, which might not be convenient for in-situ measurements. In addition, the environmental footprint of these techniques, especially with the need for preconcentration and extractive procedures, is questionable. Therefore, the detection and the discrimination of iron species remain a challenge.

Fluorescence sensors have therefore come to the scene and received considerable global recognition for their impressive sensitivity and selectivity [26,27]. Many of these label-free probes have primarily found application in clinical and food safety contexts, albeit with a small Stokes shift, probability of self-quenching, and susceptibility to photobleaching resulting in phototoxic species [8,28]. A noteworthy alternative and a new entrant in the field of nanotechnology is carbon dots (CDs), which have gained widespread use as nanoprobes for detecting various analytes [29–31]. CDs possess remarkable attributes, including their diminutive size, ease of preparation, distinct optoelectrical features, tunable fluorescence, photostability, notable biocompatibility, high water dispersibility, cost-effective production, and amenability to doping and surface passivation, making them efficient fluorescent nano-sensors. The utilization of CDs in this context has demonstrated remarkable sensitivity, with limits of detection reaching the femtomolar level, a critical feature for in-situ and real-time analyses [32–34]. Albeit with the significance of the speciation analysis specially in the environmental contexts, there has been a scarce focus on the use of CDs for the discrimination of the iron species [15,35–37]. Literature analysis showed that main emphasis has been the selective determination of the trivalent metal ions; iron (III), using CDs. This could be attributed to the strong coordination and the high quenching efficiency observed when iron (III) ions interact with CDs [38–40].

However, despite these captivating properties, the synthesis routes and the choice of carbon precursors considerably impact the morphology, size, and photoluminescence of CDs. These factors pose impediments that constrain the broad application of CDs. The reliance on hazardous chemicals, toxic precursors, and high synthesis temperatures, on the one hand, and the difficulty in controlling these harsh synthetic conditions, on the other hand, often restrict both the synthesis and practical application of CDs. Hence, it is imperative to explore green and sustainable approaches for the facile synthesis of CDs. Recently, researchers have increasingly turned their attention to natural carbon precursors derived from agricultural waste. These sources are abundant, functionally rich, easily accessible, and do not require the use of hazardous chemicals or solvents. CDs sourced from natural materials have begun to exhibit their potential as environmentally friendly alternatives. The differences in carbon content among the biomass materials, as well as variations in pyrolysis temperatures and synthetic methods, have resulted in CDs with diverse physicochemical characteristics. These differences encompass factors such as quantum yield, solubility, and stability. Therefore, it is crucial to maintain control over the synthesis process and fine-tune the sensing performance to ensure the development of highly soluble, sensitive, selective, and intensely photoluminescent CDs [41,42].

Herein, we present for the first time a streamlined one-step hydrothermal carbonization process designed for the synthesis of water-dispersible CDs. A key innovation in this method is the utilization of biochar derived from olive tree pruning biomass, pyrolyzed at various temperatures, as the carbon precursor. Through this approach, we successfully produced fluorescent CDs with a notably high quantum yield (QY ≈ 31 %), all under controlled, yet mild experimental conditions. Our findings underscored the CDs' unique ability to selectivity respond to various metal ions, exhibiting an on-off response behavior. Particularly noteworthy was the significant fluorescence quenching observed when these CDs came into contact with Fe (III) compared to Fe (II) ions, allowing the discrimination of the two iron species.

To fine-tune the sensing performance of the developed CDs, a statistical approach—the Plackett-Burman Design (PBD)—was operated. This design facilitated measurement of the fluorescence quenching efficiency as the primary response while systematically varying three crucial independent variables: pH, the nature of the reaction medium (RM), and the contact time (CT). As such, operating this design allowed for capitalizing the greenness of the current approach while harvesting its inherent benefits of conserving resources and creating data with high degree of reliability [43,44]. Having said that, the application of the developed innovative nano-sensor was extended to practical contexts by assessing their ability to test the existence of iron in actual food supplements and drinking water samples. Our research, consequently, serves as a cornerstone in capitalizing the potential of eco-friendly CDs for practical applications in analytical chemistry and environmental monitoring.

All chemicals applied herein were of high purity, were used as received. Ultrapure deionized water (DIW) was purified using the Millipore-Q system obtained from Millipore (MA, USA). Iron (III) nitrate nonahydrate, iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate, copper (II) acetate monohydrate, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, silver nitrate, lead nitrate, calcium nitrate tetrahydrate, aluminum sulfate octa decahydrate, potassium dichromate, chromium (III) chloride hexahydrate, lanthanum (III) nitrate hexahydrates, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 % w/w in water), sodium chloride, quinine sulfate, and acetonitrile (ACN) were all procured from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, USA). Ready-made phosphate buffer solutions (pH 4.0, 7.0, and 10.0) were a product of Fluka Analytical (USA).

Olive tree pruning biochar samples (BC-OTPR) were acquired in a chopped form from an Egyptian agricultural waste management firm. These biochar samples were produced at three different pyrolysis temperatures (500, 600, and 700 °C) under N2 atmosphere. Subsequently, the obtained samples were pulverized and sieved to ensure particle sizes of <10 μm. We labeled these biochar samples as BC-OTPR500, BC-OTPR600, and BC-OTPR700.

A set of 100 mL Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclaves were employed to synthesize CDs from BC-OTPR. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 1900 i, Japan) was used to determine the absorbance of CDs utilizing matched quartz cuvettes. Visualization of the fluorescent properties (qualitatively) of CDs was performed using Spectroline® CM UV-viewing mini-cabinet with 4 W removable lamps (combination UV light source; 365 nm/254 nm), Sigma-Aldrich (MO, USA). Quantitative fluorescent intensity (FI) measurements were conducted using a Shimadzu (RF-6000, Japan) spectrofluorometer equipped with a Hellma® quartz fluorescent cuvette with a path length of 10 mm. The collection of data was performed at intervals of 1 nm and a scan speed of 2000 nm/min with excitation and emission bandwidths of 5 nm. A PerkinElmer Spectrum 400 FTIR/NIR with UATR (8 scans, with a resolution of 4 cm−1, USA) was used to assess the surface functional groups. For the FTIR measurements, approximately 10 mg of the freeze-dried CDs were directly placed on the crystal surface of the UATR accessory, and the spectral data were recorded within the range from 4000 to 500 cm−1. The morphology and elemental structure of the obtained CDs were assessed using a Thermo Fisher Scientific scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 200, USA) with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDX). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a TEM (FEI Tecnai G2 S Twin FEG, USA) operated at 200 KV. ImageJ was utilized to assess the size of the prepared CDs particles. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was approached using an Axis XPS (Ultra DLD XPS Kratos), utilizing an Al Kα radiation source (1486.6 eV) with an X-ray power of 15 kV and 20 mA in a UHV environment (approximately 5 × 10−9 Torr). A reference for binding energies was established using the carbon (C-C/C-H) spectrum at 284.6 eV, with an accuracy of ±0.1 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and measurement of quantum yield (QY)
ce:section.ce:para: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from the three biochars (BC-OTPR500, BC-OTPR600, and BC-OTPR700) was performed by dispersing 1.0 g of BC-OTPR into 30.0 mL DIW. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL reaction vessel. The autoclave with the reaction components was heated in the oven at 200 °C for 4 h to synthesize CDs. The reaction vessel was left to cool down, and a filter paper followed by a 0.45 μm syringe filter were utilized to filter and purify the contents the contents. Solutions of the obtained CDs (labeled as CDs-BC-OTPR500, CDs-BC-OTPR600, and CDs-BC-OTPR700) were scanned at different excitation wavelengths (λex), and the emission wavelength (λem) was detected in the range of 350–520 nm. The excitation and emission wavelengths at which maximum FI was attained was recorded at λex = 300 nm and λem = 420 nm. Solutions of CDs were preserved at 4 °C until further use. For FTIR, SEM/EDX, and XPS analyses, samples underwent freeze-drying under vacuum for a period of 48 h.

Preliminary experiments involved assessing the quantum yield (QY) of the CDs produced from three distinct biochars. QY was measured using quinine sulfate reference solution at a concentration of 1.25 × 10−3 M dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, and the QY was computed employing Eq. (1).
(1)

Here, Q

r refer respectively to the absorbance of the synthesized CDs and the quinine sulfate standard reference solution. Additionally, both η and η
r denote the refraction index of the CDs and the standard reference quinine sulfate, where η = η

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions: selectivity testing
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesized CDs' selectivity was evaluated against several metals ions: Fe (III), Fe (II), Cu (II), Zn (II), Ca (II), Pb (II), La (III), Cr (III), Cr (VI), Ag (I), and Al (III). In this regard, stock solutions of 10 mg/L of the metals were prepared in DIW. In general, 500.0 μL of CDs-BC-OTPR500 solution was inserted into the fluorescence cuvette, followed by 2.0 mL of DIW, 500.0 μL of buffer (pH = 7.00 ± 0.02) and 500.0 μL of the metal ion standard solution. The mixture was stirred and allowed to stand for 10 min at room temperature. The fluorescence quenching capability of the different metal ions was employed as a tool to evaluate the synthesized CDs' selectivity, and the quenching effect was calculated as F 0/F, where
F 0
denotes the FI of CDs-BC-OTPR500, while F signifies the FI at 420 nm with iron (III) present. All measurements were performed against a reagent blank where all constituents of the reaction were there, omitting the metal ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of iron using BC-OTPR500–based fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para:
PBD was used to optimize the iron (III) detection conditions. The objective was set to have the highest sensitivity (maximum fluorescence quenching, F 0/F). PBD is best described by the polynomial model displayed in Eq. (2) [45–47].
(2)
Y =
β 0
+ Σ
β i
x i

Here, Y is the response variable, F 0/F, while β0 represents the intercept of the mean, x
i is the independent variable affecting the response, and βi is the corresponding coefficient. A thorough analysis was conducted considering three variables: contact time (CT), pH, and the medium of the reaction (RM), Table 1 . The design scenario entailed 20 base runs conducted as 2 replicates (total runs: 40) over 2 blocks with a total of 16 central points, Table S1. A 179 μM stock solution of iron (III) was made in DIW, and serial dilutions were concocted in the same solvent. To depict the experimental scenario, an aliquot of 500.0 μL of CDs-BC-OTPR500 was placed into the 3.5 mL cuvette followed by 2.0 mL of the RM (DIW, or ACN), 500.0 μL buffer solution (pH is decided as shown in Table S1), and 500.0 μL of iron (III) stock solution. The reaction mixture was kept for 30 s – 10 min (abiding by the frame shown in Table S1). All measurements were done in triplicates and were measured versus reagent blanks similarly prepared, omitting iron (III). To construct the calibration curve, the same procedure was followed after deciding upon the optimum conditions so that the final concentrations of iron (III) are in the range of 1.186 μM – 7.984 μM.

For the detection of iron (II), a similar procedure was followed, however, using 21 μL of H2O2 as an oxidizing agent to convert iron (II) into iron (III). The optimum conditions for detecting iron (III) were then followed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of iron in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the analysis of iron content, we collected various real samples, including drinking water (with iron (II) content labeled to be ˂ 0.02 mg/L) and iron food supplements (Fe Food Supplement, labeled to contain 60 mg of ferrous sulfate per tablet in addition to other ingredients, including stabilizer microcrystalline cellulose, polyvinyl pyrrolidone, and anti-coking magnesium salts of fatty acids and silicon dioxide). These samples were purchased from local hypermarkets and pharmacy stores, respectively. The concentration of iron (III) in different matrices was determined using the method of standard addition. This procedure included the addition of 200.0 μL of each sample, along with 500.0 μL of CDs-BC-OTPR500 and 500.0 μL of buffer (pH = 10.0 ± 0.2), and then 2 mL of ACN to a 3.5 mL quartz cuvette. Subsequently, different concentrations of iron (III) standard solution were added to have final iron (III) concentrations between 1.15 and 5.74 μM. Samples were left for 10 min at room temperature. Fluorescence intensity was assessed at an excitation wavelength (λex) of 300 nm, and the emissions were detected at 420 nm (λem).

In the current investigation, the production of CDs was effectively performed using an agro-waste biochar precursor via a hydrothermal synthetic route. The prepared CDs were effectively dispersed in DIW, resulting in a clear solution. UV–Vis spectra were employed to characterize the synthesized CDs, as portrayed in Fig. 1 . These spectra revealed a distinct peak in the range of 240–244 nm for the three CDs-BC-OTPR. This peak might be related to the π-π* transition of the CC and CC aromatic bonds arising from the aromatic π-system of the BC-OTPR biochar [46]. The prepared CDs-BC-OTPR500 exhibited a light blue luminescence when exposed to a UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm, as revealed in the inset of Fig. 1. Upon irradiation with the UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm, CDs-BC-OTPR500 displayed strong light blue fluorescence, likely due to the increased nitrogen and carbon content within the CDs-BC-OTPR500 structure compared to the other two samples [45].

The choice of the carbon source for synthesizing CDs involved testing three different biochar samples: BC-OTPR500, BC-OTPR600, and BC-OTPR700. The comparison of the CDs derived from these biochar samples was primarily based on their quantum yield (QY), as presented in Table 2 . Among these samples, CDs-BC-OTPR500 exhibited the highest QY, 31.01 %. This outcome agrees with previous research findings indicating that biochar synthesized below 400 °C may undergo incomplete carbonization [48]. Additionally, the results revealed that the QY decreased when the pyrolysis temperature increased from 500 to 700 °C. This observation aligns with prior reports that noted a reduction in photoluminescence as the pyrolysis temperature was elevated [49]. Consequently, CDs-BC-OTPR500 was selected for further investigations.

It is worth highlighting that the QY achieved in this study far exceeds the QY reported in previous investigations involving feedstock-derived CDs, even without any doping. Table 3 provides a comparison between CDs-BC-OTPR500, and CDs derived from various biomass and biochar sources.

The TEM images shown in Fig. 2 illustrate the particle size and morphology of the as-prepared CDs. The TEM image in Fig. 2(a–e) showed a high-density population of CDs with the majority of particles possessing a uniform spherical shape in the case of CDs-BC-OTPR500 and CDs-BC-OTPR600 [45,47]. Conversely, the inset in Fig. 2(b) reveals the existence of lattice fringes in the CDs, which reveals the graphitic nature of the CDs. In addition, the inset displays an inner core consisting of tiny dots measuring 0.22 nm, surrounded by an outer shell that has an amorphous structure, which is a typical feature of the CDs [59,60]. The histogram of the particle size distribution, Fig. 2(f), reveals that the particle size of CDs-BC-OTPR500 is 12.07 ± 2.53 nm. CDs-BC-OTPR600, Fig. 2(g), exhibited an average particle size of 15.56 ± 3.57 nm, along with some instances of agglomerated particles. In contrast, the TEM images of CDs-BC-OTPR700, Fig. 2(e) displayed a higher degree of agglomeration, which negatively affected the QY of the produced CDs. This increase in the particle size from 500 to 700 °C could be attributed to agglomeration encountered with the increase in the pyrolysis temperature. This comes in conformity with the results of QY measurements displayed in Table 2, and with the findings of Mejía Ávila et al., where the increase in pyrolysis temperature is accompanied by a decrease in photoluminescence [49].

Furthermore, SEM micrographs in Fig. 2(f–i) showed the presence of CDs with a tubular structure following the freeze-drying process. This tubular structure was particularly prominent in CDs-BC-OTPR500, Fig. 2(f, g). However, in CDs-BC-OTPR600, Fig. 2(h), particles began to agglomerate (in agreement with the TEM findings), and this aggregation was even more pronounced in CDs-BC-OTPR700, Fig. 2(i). The EDX analysis was used to assess the elemental composition of the as-prepared CDs, as shown in Fig. 2(j–l). The carbon concentration decreased from 13.81 % in CDs-BC-OTPR500 to 11.38 % in CDs-BC-OTPR700, confirming the higher carbon content in CDs-BC-OTPR500, which positively impacted the QY. The analysis indicated that the second major component of the produced CDs is oxygen, with concentration increasing from 45.36 % to 53.03 % for CDs-BC-OTPR500 and CDs-BC-OTPR700, respectively, which could be formed during the drying process of the as-prepared CDs. Additionally, the analysis showed a high concentration of potassium (K) and low concentrations of other elements, including Na, Mg, S, and Cl.

FTIR spectra, Fig. 3 , were utilized to investigate the functional groups present on the surface of the synthesized CDs. The obtained absorption spectra of the three synthesized CDs showed a prominent and broad absorption peak at 3300 cm−1, signifying OH stretching vibrations mode and the presence of water with the acidic functional groups vibrations resulting in a wider band [61]. Two additional absorption bands at 2981 cm−1 for the CH2 antisymmetric stretching vibrations and the band at 2895 cm−1 revealed the existence of CH2 symmetric stretching vibrations [62]. The absorption band at 1741 cm−1 corresponds to the presence of CO stretching vibrations. Furthermore, a strong absorption band at 1622 cm−1 signifies the presence of the carboxylate group or the CC stretching vibrations and confirms the existence of sp
2 structures in the as-synthesized CDs [63,64]. The shoulder bands at 1582 and 1388 cm−1, reveal the presence of NH deformation and the stretching vibrations of CN amide III, respectively. Conversely, a strong peak is consistently observed at 1367 cm−1 in all samples, indicating CN stretching vibrations. The aromatic CH in-plane bending vibration was confirmed by the presence of vibration bands at 1183 and 1119 cm−1. The absorption bands between 1000 and 675 cm−1 suggest the presence of aromatic CH out-plane bending vibrations located in the FTIR spectrum at 970, 829, 780, and 706 cm−1 [61,65–67]. Moreover, the presence of absorption bands between 1250 and 1000 cm−1 proves the presence of CO stretching in phenols or alcohols and confirms the aromaticity of the synthesized CDs. Additionally, weak peaks located at 1119 cm−1 and 618 cm−1 correspond to the in-plane and out-of-plane bending band of OH of the carboxylic acids [68].

The structural features revealed by the FTIR spectra of the CDs could be explained by the multi-stage transformation process that BC-OTPR typically undergoes during the hydrothermal treatment. This process includes removing water and subsequent condensation reactions, leading to the formation of polymeric structures, followed by carbonization or aromatization under elevated temperatures and pressures. These processes result in the creation of carbon skeletons with sp
2 carbon structures reminiscent of benzene rings [69]. Simultaneously, various surface functional groups, such as hydroxyl groups (–OH), carboxylic acid groups (–COOH), and amino groups (−NH2), are formed on the surface of the CDs. The hydrothermal process, which uses hot pressurized water to penetrate the BC-OTPR's structure, plays a crucial role in these transformations, particularly in converting crystalline cellulose to an amorphous state and the rapid formation of oligomers [70]. Hemicellulose, a lightweight component, decomposes at 160 °C [71]. Furthermore, lignin might undergo wet oxidation under elevated temperatures and high pressure. In the process of wet oxidation of lignin, aromatic aldehydes are produced through the oxidation of carbon into CO and CO [72]. These structural characteristics make CDs unique nanomaterials with potential applications in sensing and fluorescence.

The analysis of the elemental composition and chemical state of the CDs synthesized from BC-OTPR was carried out using the XPS technique, as shown in Fig. 4 (a–c) for CDs-BC-OTPR500, Fig. 4(d–f) for CDs-OTPR600 and Fig. 4(g–i) for CDs-BC-OTPR700. The resulting survey spectrum for the three CDs samples revealed the presence of three distinct signal peaks at 283.6, 397.9, and 530.6 eV, corresponding to the elements C, N, and O, respectively [73]. The relative atomic ratios for the as-prepared CDs-BC-OTPR500 were found to be 66.82 % for C, 2.91 % for N, and 30.27 % for O. In contrast, the relative atomic ratios for the other two CDs samples were 65.73 % and 61.58 % for C, 0.71 % and 9.40 % for N, and 33.56 % and 29.02 % for O, for CDs-BC-OTPR600 and CDs-BC-OTPR700, respectively. Notably, CDs-BC-OTPR500 exhibited the highest carbon content among the three samples, which is a crucial factor potentially influencing the QY of the CDs.

Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s and O1s regions (Fig. 4) provided detailed information about the chemical bonding on the surface of the as-synthesized CDs. The C1s spectra showed the presence of various carbon species, including CC, CC, C–OH, and CO bonds, as evidenced by the peaks at binding energies of 282.5, 283.9, 285.1, and 286.6 eV, respectively [73,74]. The O1s XPS spectra indicated the existence of oxygen in different chemical states, OH, CO, and CO bonds through three fitted peaks appearing at 530.1, 530.5, and 530.9 eV, respectively [75–77]. These XPS findings align with the FTIR results and provide additional confirmation of the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen moieties on the surface of the CDs, highlighting their complex surface chemistry and potential for diverse applications.

The photostability of CDs-BC-OTPR500 was investigated by noting the change in FI over a time of 3 h (continuous irradiation). The resulting data showed that CDs-BC-OTPR500 possess a high photostability, Fig. 5(a). Additionally, the changes in the FI in response to varying concentrations of NaCl were monitored.

As illustrated in Fig. 5(b), no significant changes were observed, even at a NaCl concentration of 0.8 M, demonstrating the exceptional stability of CDs in high ionic strength conditions. These findings demonstrate the potential of utilizing CDs as sensors. On the other hand, the effect of pH on the FI of the CDs was investigated in a pH range from 4.0 to 10.0. The FI of the CDs gradually increases with rising pH from 4.0 to 10.0, indicating their pH sensitivity, Fig. 5(c, d). These findings demonstrate the possibility of utilizing CDs-BC-OTPR500 as sensors for a longer time and in the presence of high ionic strength.

CDs-BC-OTPR500 showed fluorescence emission properties that depend on excitation, enabling wavelength-tunable emission. When exposed to UV light, the emission wavelength displayed fluctuations from 280 to 300 nm, as shown in Fig. 6(a). Changing the excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 400 nm caused a red shift in emission intensity. The CDs-BC-OTPR500 were optimally excited at 300 nm, providing the highest emission intensity. Fig. 6(b–d) also illustrates that adding various metal ions quenched the fluorescence of CDs-BC-OTPR500 to varying extents. Nickel (II) induced a slight decrease, copper (II) resulted in a more pronounced decrease, while iron (II) caused a substantial drop in the FI, exceeding 50 %. Remarkably, iron (III) exhibited even greater quenching potential compared to chromium (VI) and iron (II).

The presence of different functional groups, such as −OH, −NH, and C − O, on the surface of CDs-BC-OTPR500, as confirmed by XPS and FTIR analyses, facilitated the coordination with iron (III) ions. This coordination led to a strong fluorescence-quenching effect by enabling the transfer of the excited state electrons from the CDs-BC-OTPR500 to the unoccupied 4 s orbital of iron (III), resulting in non-radiative electron/hole recombination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of factors influencing Iron (III) detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The current study applied PBD to illustrate the correlation between the response (F 0/F) and key independent variables: CT, pH, RM. The polynomial model outlined in Eq. (3) was operated to fit the experimental data. Box-Cox response transformation [78] using a transformation factor, λ = 0 was performed, and the following regression equation was obtained:
(3)
ln
F 0
F
= 0.166805 + 0.003423
CT + 0.013572
pH + 0.060446
RM − 0.109484
Ct
Pt

In Eq. (3), ‘Ct Pt’ denotes the central point, and it could be observed that all studied variables have positively influenced the response, with RM possessing the largest magnitude. The reliability of the proposed model was further confirmed by the values of R2 (0.9997) and the adjusted R2 (0.9997), reflecting the high agreement between the experimental findings and the model's predictions, which is also further substantiated by the low values of % error displayed in Table S1. The predicted R2 (0.9996) was also high, confirming the model's capability to predict any new observation. Furthermore, the value of predicted R2 was not substantially lower than R2, corroborating that the model was not over-fit.

The analysis of variance (ANOVA) indicated that the three variables displayed a p-value of <0.05, confirming their statistical significance. On the other hand, the lack-of-fit had a p-value >0.05 (insignificant), corroborating that the model did not encounter any lack-of-fit.

The statistical significance of the tested variables was further verified using the Pareto chart, Fig. 7(a). As could be observed, tested variables surpass the reference threshold, affirming their impact on the response. The data shown in Fig. 7(b) portrays the 2D contour plots, which were utilized to explore the relation between the response F 0/F and two of the predictor factors, pH and CT, represented respectively on the x- and y-axes. The dark red contours manifest a region where the fluorescence quenching is minimal, and the pH is between 6.5 and 7.5, while the CT is between 4.5 and 6.5 min. Furthermore, the 3D surface plots were applied to evaluate the relation between these two factors, yet in a 3D format where the z-axis represents the response F 0/F. Data in Fig. 7(c) indicates that the degree of quenching, represented by the high ridges, could be maximized at pH = 10 and for CT = 10 min.

The individual desirability function (d) was utilized to identify the ideal experimental setup for maximizing the response. Desirability plot – Figure is not shown – revealed that maximum fluorescence quenching could be attained using a factorial blend of CT = 10 min, pH = 10 and a RM of ACN. These optimum conditions were further used in the construction of the calibration curve and the standard addition technique.

In the current investigation, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-BC-OTPR500 was significantly quenched in presence of iron (III) ions whereas no such effect is observed with iron (II) ions, as previously shown in Fig. 6(b–d), implying a fast, sensitive, and selective behavior towards iron (III). On the other hand, the detection of iron (II) was also feasible following its oxidation into iron (III) using H2O2 via Fenton reaction, as demonstrated by Eq. (4):
(4)
Fe
2 +
+
H 2
O 2
→
Fe
3 +
+ OH ˙ +
OH −
This reaction can be employed for the selective determination of iron (II) by converting it to iron (III) and then detecting iron (III) using CDs-BC-OTPR500.

To study the influence of H2O2 concentration on the FI of CDs-BC-OTPR500 and its effect on the detection of iron (II), 45 μM of iron (II) was prepared, and different concentrations of H2O2 were added. As illustrated in Fig. 8(a), the FI of the CDs decreases in proportionality with the increased H2O2 concentrations. The optimal concentration of H2O2 is determined when there is no significant fluorescence intensity, which means a complete oxidation of iron (II) to iron (III). Different H2O2 concentrations were tested to find the optimal level for oxidizing the maximum concentration of iron (II) within a linear range up to 45 μM. The selected concentration was 60 mM H2O2, Fig. 8(b), based on achieving complete iron (II) oxidation while maintaining stable fluorescence of the CDs-BC-OTPR500.

The obtained calibration curve for iron (III) (and iron (II) following the oxidation using H2O2) determination using CDs-BC-OTPR500 demonstrated a linear in the range of 1.186 μM − 7.984 μM at excitation and emission wavelengths of 300 and 420 nm, respectively. This linear relationship is expressed by Eqs. (5) and (6) as follows:
(5)
F 0
/ F = 0.0710
C + 0.9557 ,
R 2
= 0.9991
(6)
F 0
/ F = 0.0848
C + 0.9371 ,
R 2
= 0.9973

In these equations, C denotes the concentration of iron (III) and iron (II) following the oxidation with H2O2, respectively. The obtained coefficient of determination, R2 value is high, indicating the linearity of the collected data. A summary of the analytical data can be found in Table 3. Sensitivity was assessed through the determination of the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) using Eqs. (7) and (8), correspondingly:
(7)
LOD = 3
SD a
(8)
LOQ = 10 ×
SD a

In this context, SD stands for the standard deviation of the blank CDs-BC-OTPR500, and ‘a’ represents the slope of the straight line in the calibration curve (n = 4). Within a linear range of 1.186 μM − 7.984 μM, the LOD of iron (III) is 0.5 μM, and the LOQ is 1.6 μM, compared to a LOD of 1.4 μM, and LOQ of 4.5 μM in the case of iron (II) following its oxidation using H2O2. In the case of iron (II) and in the absence of H2O2, a less pronounced quenching effect on CDs-BC-OTPR500 compared to iron (III) was observed. The calibration curve in this case was linear in the range of 5.776 μM – 33.878 μM with LOD of 7.4 μM and the LOQ of 24.8 μM. These values demonstrate the high sensitivity of the as-prepared CDs towards iron. Moreover, the LOD set by the current approach is a way lower compared to the limits considered by the guiding lines of the World Health Organization (WHO) and the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) where a maximum of 0.3 mg/L (5.537 μM) of iron in drinking water is permitted [6,35].

The accuracy and precision were measured at a 95.0 % confidence interval (CI) for the determination of iron in the authentic samples. The obtained data showed that the %mean recovery for iron (III) was 100.29 ± 0.01 compared to 99.94 ± 0.04 in the case of iron (II) after oxidation, confirming the accuracy of the proposed method. The obtained values of percentage relative standard deviation (%RSD) were <1 %, confirming the high precision.

Compared to the other CQDs-based sensors that have been applied for the speciation of iron, the current approach shows values that are superior or comparable to the LOD reported for both iron species, Table 4 .

CDs' fluorescence quenching can be classified into two main mechanisms: dynamic and static quenching. Dynamic quenching arises from collisions between the CDs and quencher molecules, whereas static quenching results from forming a complex between the CDs and the quenching agent [80]. To identify the quenching mechanism of iron (III) on the synthesized CDs-BC-OTPR500, the Stern-Volmer equation was employed to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism. This relationship can be expressed as F 0/F = 1 + K
sv [Fe3+], where
F 0
signifies the FI of CDs-BC-OTPR500, while F indicates the FI with iron (III) present, while the K
sv indicates the quenching constant. As depicted in Fig. S1(a), with the temperature of the solution being raised from 25 °C to 60 °C, the K
sv values showed a marked decrease from 0.065 μM−1 to 0.054 μM−1, which could be attributed to the recovery of fluorescence due to the reduced stability of the resulting ground-state complex, suggesting that static quenching may be occurring between the prepared CDs-BC-OTPR500 and iron (III) [81,82]. Furthermore, UV–vis absorption spectra were employed to confirm the quenching mechanism. As evidenced in Fig. S1(b), upon adding iron (III), the absorption spectrum of the added up absorption spectrum of CDs-BC-OTPR500 plus iron (III) did not match the absorption spectrum of the resultant iron (III)/CDs-BC-OTPR500, which may be due to the interaction between iron (III) ions and CDs-BC-OTPR500 through the coordination reaction between different functional groups, such as −OH, −NH, and C − O, on the surface of CDs-BC-OTPR500 and iron (III) ions [81]. Hence, the fluorescence quenching of CDs-BC-OTPR500 in the presence of iron (III) ions can be attributed to the static quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of iron (II) and iron (III) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For real samples, two iron (II) samples including drinking water and iron (II) food supplement were tested for iron (III) content as well as iron (II) after adding H2O2 (60 mM). The accuracy was validated from the recovery experiments following the spiking of the real samples with standard iron (II) solution, Table 5 . Generally, the % recoveries of iron (II) and iron (III) ranged between 93.3 % to 108.3, Table 5, reflecting a consistently high level of accuracy. These satisfactory results suggest that the matrix, which often includes other interfering components, has only a minimal effect on the performance of the current sensor in detecting iron (II) and iron (III).

The method precision has been further assessed by inter- and intra-day precision measurements for three replicate measurements of three different concentrations over three days and on the same day, respectively, Table S2. The proposed procedure shows excellent precision for both authentic and real samples as reflected by the low %RSD values (˂ 5 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Eco-scale assessment of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: The data presented in Table 6 compares the greenness of the proposed method with that of the reported method by Yaman and Kaya, using the eco-scale assessment [83]. This assessment evaluates the environmental impact of laboratory procedures based on the penalty points (PPs) assigned to various reagents, instruments, and hazards [84]. The obtained data shows that the proposed method uses fewer and less hazardous reagents and involves fewer risks, resulting in only 11 total PPs with an eco-scale score of 89, indicating a more environmentally friendly approach. In contrast, the reported flame atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) method used for the speciation of both iron (II) and iron (III) uses potentially much of hazardous chemicals for the preconcentration process, equipment that consumes energy, and has higher occupational hazards and waste, leading to a higher total PPs of 36 with a lower eco-scale score of 64, making it less environment friendly. The lower the PPs and the higher the eco-scale score of the proposed methods makes it a greener method.

In conclusion, this study presents a simple, green, and cost-effective approach for the detection and discrimination of iron using Carbon Dots (CDs) derived from olive tree pruning (OTPR) biochar. The synthesized CDs exhibited remarkable performance in the determination of iron (III) and iron (II) concentrations using a Fenton system in various real samples, including food supplements and drinking water samples, with excellent precision and recovery rates. Among the various biochar precursors, CDs synthesized from OTPR biochar pyrolyzed at 500 °C (CDs-BC-OTPR500) exhibited exceptional luminescent properties, with a high quantum yield (QY) of 31.01 %. This study has demonstrated that the selection of the carbon precursor and the pyrolysis temperature significantly impacts the CDs' luminescent properties, and lower pyrolysis temperatures can result in higher QY.

The CDs derived from CDs-BC-OTPR500 were shown to have excitation-dependent fluorescence, allowing for wavelength-tunable emission with optimal excitation at 300 nm. These CDs also exhibited excellent sensitivity to iron ions, particularly iron (III), making them suitable for metal ion detection. The proposed CDs-BC-OTPR500-based method displayed a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.5 μM and a high level of accuracy, as confirmed through calibration curves and standard addition techniques.

The analytical method developed in this study is sensitive, accurate, and versatile, demonstrating the potential of CDs-BC-OTPR500 in applications related to metal ion sensing and discrimination. Overall, the study underscores the significance of biochar-based carbon dots as advanced materials for various analytical and environmental applications.
